questions.stem: A 79-year-old patient on a medical unit is found to have a 5 cm × 4 cm sacral wound with full-thickness tissue loss, visible subcutaneous fat, and partial slough coverage of the wound base. No bone, tendon, or muscle is visible. What is the correct NPUAP/EPUAP pressure injury stage and priority nursing intervention?

options.text: Stage 1; increase repositioning frequency and apply a transparent film dressing
Stage 3; apply a moisture-retentive dressing, reposition every 2 hours off the sacrum, consult wound care specialist, and address nutrition
Stage 4; immediate surgical debridement is required before any wound care intervention
Unstageable; cover with dry gauze until slough completely resolves before determining stage or treatment

questions.rationale: NPUAP/EPUAP Pressure Injury Stages: Stage 1 — non-blanchable erythema, intact skin; Stage 2 — partial thickness dermis loss, shallow open ulcer; Stage 3 — full-thickness skin loss with visible subcutaneous fat, may have slough, but no bone/tendon/muscle visible; Stage 4 — full-thickness with exposed bone, tendon, or muscle; Unstageable — base entirely obscured by non-removable slough or eschar. This wound is Stage 3 (full-thickness, subcutaneous fat visible, partial slough but bone/tendon not exposed). Management: moist wound healing (foam, hydrocolloid, or alginate dressings based on exudate level), reposition every 2 hours off the sacrum, pressure redistribution support surface, wound care specialist consultation, nutritional assessment (protein, zinc, vitamin C support wound healing). Dry gauze is contraindicated — desiccates wound bed and impairs healing. Slough does not make a wound automatically unstageable (unstageable requires the base to be completely obscured).

distractorRationales.a: Selecting Stage 1; increase repositioning frequency and apply a transparent film dressing would be unsafe, rather than acting on Stage 3 — full-thickness skin loss with visible subcutaneous fat, may have slough. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The safer answer is Stage 3; apply a moisture-retentive dressing, reposition every 2 hours off the sacrum, consult wound care because delaying infection or sepsis escalation can worsen infection progression.
distractorRationales.c: This distractor pulls attention toward Stage 4; immediate surgical debridement is required before any wound care intervention, but the stem cue is Treat with moist wound dressings, 2-hour repositioning, pressure redistribution, nutrition support. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so It is less defensible because Stage 3; apply a moisture-retentive dressing, reposition every 2 hours off the sacrum, consult wound care targets the problem most likely to deteriorate first.
distractorRationales.d: This distractor pulls attention toward Unstageable; cover with dry gauze until slough completely resolves before determining stage or treatment, but the stem cue is Treat with moist wound dressings, 2-hour repositioning, pressure redistribution, nutrition support. Moves to a lower-acuity action before the client is clinically stable, so It is less defensible because Stage 3; apply a moisture-retentive dressing, reposition every 2 hours off the sacrum, consult wound care targets the problem most likely to deteriorate first.

questions.takeaway: Stage 3 pressure injury: full-thickness, subcutaneous fat visible, no bone/tendon. Treat with moist wound dressings, 2-hour repositioning, pressure redistribution, nutrition support, and wound care consult. Dry gauze contraindicated.

questions.stem: A nurse picks up a syringe that a previous nurse prepared and left in the medication room labeled 'Patient Jones — morphine 4 mg.' The nurse's patient is Jones and the morphine dose is ordered. Which action should the nurse take?

options.text: Administer the pre-drawn syringe since it is labeled with the correct patient name and drug
Discard the pre-drawn syringe and prepare a new dose from the medication dispensing system
Ask the previous nurse to verify the dose by phone before administering
Document the medication as given and monitor the patient closely for response

questions.rationale: Pre-drawn, unlabeled (or hand-labeled) syringes prepared by another nurse and left in a medication room present serious patient safety risks: the medication, dose, or preparation process cannot be independently verified by the administering nurse. Per safety standards (Joint Commission, ISMP), nurses should never administer a medication that they did not personally prepare and verify from a labeled, system-dispensed source. The pre-drawn syringe should be discarded and a fresh dose prepared directly from the medication dispensing cabinet, allowing the nurse to independently verify the drug, dose, concentration, expiration, and route. Phone verification by the previous nurse is insufficient — the administering nurse bears full accountability.

distractorRationales.a: This option makes Administer the pre-drawn syringe since it is labeled with the correct patient name and drug the focus, even though the higher-risk finding is ' The nurse's patient is Jones and the morphine dose is ordered. Anchors on the wrong explanation for the client's cues, so Discard the pre-drawn syringe and prepare a new dose from the medication dispensing system better matches the nursing priority, so this option would slow the needed intervention.
distractorRationales.c: Ask the previous nurse to verify the dose by phone before administering is not the best answer, and misses that Per safety standards (Joint Commission, ISMP), nurses should never administer a medication that they did not personally prepare. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so Discard the pre-drawn syringe and prepare a new dose from the medication dispensing system is safer because it addresses medication toxicity before routine care or reassurance.
distractorRationales.d: This choice focuses on Document the medication as given and monitor the patient closely for response, instead of responding to ' The nurse's patient is Jones and the morphine dose is ordered. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The nurse should prioritize Discard the pre-drawn syringe and prepare a new dose from the medication dispensing system; otherwise medication toxicity remains the active threat.

questions.takeaway: Never administer a medication drawn up by another nurse. You cannot verify what is in that syringe. Discard and prepare your own dose. This applies in all settings. The 'rights' of medication administration include right verification — and that starts with you preparing the medication yourself.

questions.stem: An RN delegates to an LPN/LVN. Which task is within the LPN's scope of practice in most states?

options.text: Perform the initial nursing admission assessment for a newly admitted patient
Administer oral, IM, and subcutaneous medications and monitor response
Develop and update the nursing care plan based on patient assessment findings
Interpret cardiac rhythm strips and adjust IV vasopressor infusions per protocol

questions.rationale: LPN/LVN scope of practice typically includes: administering medications (oral, IM, SC, topical), performing basic wound care, collecting specimens, and monitoring and reporting patient status. Initial assessments (A) require RN-level nursing judgment and are an RN responsibility. Care plan development (C) is an RN function. Interpreting arrhythmias and adjusting vasopressors (D) requires advanced critical care RN knowledge. The key distinction is that LPNs implement care under RN supervision; RNs assess, plan, evaluate, and teach.

distractorRationales.a: This option makes Perform the initial nursing admission assessment for a newly admitted patient the focus, when the bedside priority is driven by LPN scope: administer medications, perform basic wound care, collect data, implement established care — initial assessment, care planning. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so That choice could make the nurse miss the window for infection or sepsis escalation while infection progression progresses.
distractorRationales.c: Develop and update the nursing care plan based on patient assessment findings would not address the priority, and misses that LPN scope: administer medications, perform basic wound care, collect data, implement established care — initial assessment, care planning. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The correct path is Administer oral, IM, and subcutaneous medications and monitor response, which matches the priority cues instead of a secondary concern.
distractorRationales.d: Interpret cardiac rhythm strips and adjust IV vasopressor infusions per protocol would not address the priority, without accounting for LPN scope: administer medications, perform basic wound care, collect data, implement established care — initial assessment, care planning. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The correct path is Administer oral, IM, and subcutaneous medications and monitor response, which matches the priority cues instead of a secondary concern.

questions.takeaway: LPN scope: administer medications, perform basic wound care, collect data, implement established care — initial assessment, care planning, and teaching are RN responsibilities.

questions.stem: A client reports severe eye pain, blurred vision, and seeing halos around lights. Which interpretation by the nurse is most accurate?

options.text: The client may have acute angle-closure glaucoma and needs urgent evaluation.
This is an expected effect of routine eye fatigue.
The client is likely having a migraine and can rest at home.
These findings show that prescribed eye drops are working well.

questions.rationale: Severe eye pain with blurred vision and halos is concerning for acute angle-closure glaucoma, an ocular emergency that can threaten vision. The nurse should recognize the need for urgent ophthalmic evaluation rather than dismissing the symptoms as routine strain. Time-sensitive treatment matters for outcome.

distractorRationales.b: This is an expected effect of routine eye fatigue is not the best answer, without accounting for A client reports severe eye pain, blurred vision. Treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, so That choice could make the nurse miss the window for bedside safety action while clinical safety risk progresses.
distractorRationales.c: This choice focuses on The client is likely having a migraine and can rest at home, while the decisive cue is Eye pain plus halos and blurred vision should trigger concern for acute angle-closure glaucoma. Moves to a lower-acuity action before the client is clinically stable, so The safer answer is The client may have acute angle-closure glaucoma and needs urgent evaluation because delaying bedside safety action can worsen clinical safety risk.
distractorRationales.d: This answer choice treats These findings show that prescribed eye drops are working well as the key issue, despite the clinical warning in Severe eye pain with blurred vision and halos is concerning for acute angle-closure glaucoma, an ocular emergency. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so Choosing it can delay bedside safety action and leave clinical safety risk unresolved.

questions.takeaway: Eye pain plus halos and blurred vision should trigger concern for acute angle-closure glaucoma.

questions.stem: A client with adrenal insufficiency has vomiting, weakness, blood pressure 82/48 mmHg, and sodium 128 mEq/L. Which provider prescription should the nurse implement first?

options.text: Administer IV hydrocortisone and isotonic fluids.
Restrict oral fluids and monitor weight.
Hold corticosteroids until the morning cortisol level is drawn.
Teach the client about low-sodium foods.

questions.rationale: Hypotension, hyponatremia, vomiting, and weakness in a client with adrenal insufficiency suggest adrenal crisis. The priority is rapid steroid replacement and volume support to prevent circulatory collapse. Delaying treatment for further testing can be life-threatening.

distractorRationales.b: This distractor pulls attention toward Restrict oral fluids and monitor weight, without accounting for sodium 128 mEq/L. Moves to a lower-acuity action before the client is clinically stable, so Administer IV hydrocortisone and isotonic fluids better matches the nursing priority, so this option would slow the needed intervention.
distractorRationales.c: The reasoning trap in Hold corticosteroids until the morning cortisol level is drawn is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, when the bedside priority is driven by The priority is rapid steroid replacement and volume support to prevent circulatory collapse. The correct path is Administer IV hydrocortisone and isotonic fluids, which matches the priority cues instead of a secondary concern.
distractorRationales.d: This answer choice treats Teach the client about low-sodium foods as the key issue, rather than acting on Hypotension, hyponatremia, vomiting. Moves to teaching or reassurance before the immediate risk is stabilized, so The safer answer is Administer IV hydrocortisone and isotonic fluids because delaying renal protection can worsen renal injury.

questions.takeaway: Suspected adrenal crisis requires steroids and isotonic fluids right away.

questions.stem: A client receiving IV antibiotics suddenly develops wheezing, widespread hives, and swelling of the lips. Which action should the nurse take first?

options.text: Stop the infusion and prepare to administer epinephrine.
Obtain a full allergy history before intervening.
Slow the infusion to reduce the reaction.
Document the event and continue monitoring.

questions.rationale: Wheezing, hives, and lip swelling indicate anaphylaxis. The causative infusion should be stopped immediately and epinephrine readied or administered per protocol because airway compromise and shock can develop quickly. Delaying definitive treatment can be fatal.

distractorRationales.b: This choice focuses on Obtain a full allergy history before intervening, while the decisive cue is A client receiving IV antibiotics suddenly develops wheezing, widespread hives. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so Choosing it can delay airway and breathing intervention and leave oxygenation or ventilation unresolved.
distractorRationales.c: This choice focuses on Slow the infusion to reduce the reaction, while the decisive cue is A client receiving IV antibiotics suddenly develops wheezing, widespread hives. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The nurse should prioritize Stop the infusion and prepare to administer epinephrine; otherwise oxygenation or ventilation remains the active threat.
distractorRationales.d: This distractor pulls attention toward Document the event and continue monitoring, but the stem cue is The causative infusion should be stopped immediately and epinephrine readied or administered per protocol because airway compromise. Treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, so Stop the infusion and prepare to administer epinephrine is safer because it addresses oxygenation or ventilation before routine care or reassurance.

questions.takeaway: When anaphylaxis begins, stop the trigger and act for airway and circulation immediately.

questions.stem: A client with a T4 spinal cord injury suddenly reports a pounding headache and has a blood pressure of 208/110 mmHg. Which nursing action is the priority?

options.text: Sit the client upright and assess for bladder distention.
Lay the client flat and increase the room temperature.
Ask the client to rest quietly until the headache passes.
Offer oral fluids and reassess in 30 minutes.

questions.rationale: Severe hypertension and headache in a client with a high spinal cord injury suggest autonomic dysreflexia, often triggered by bladder distention. The nurse should sit the client upright to lower blood pressure and remove the triggering stimulus right away. Delay increases the risk of stroke or seizure.

distractorRationales.b: This choice focuses on Lay the client flat and increase the room temperature, rather than acting on A client with a T4 spinal cord injury suddenly reports a pounding headache and has a blood pressure. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The priority is Sit the client upright and assess for bladder distention, not a step that delays stabilization of neurologic deterioration.
distractorRationales.c: The reasoning trap in Ask the client to rest quietly until the headache passes is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, even though the higher-risk finding is Delay increases the risk of stroke or seizure. The correct path is Sit the client upright and assess for bladder distention, which matches the priority cues instead of a secondary concern.
distractorRationales.d: The problem with Offer oral fluids and reassess in 30 minutes is that it treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, instead of responding to The nurse should sit the client upright to lower blood pressure and remove the triggering stimulus right away. The priority is Sit the client upright and assess for bladder distention, not a step that delays stabilization of neurologic deterioration.

questions.takeaway: Autonomic dysreflexia is a remove-the-trigger emergency, often starting with sitting the client up and checking the bladder.

questions.stem: A nurse begins a packed red blood cell transfusion for a client with postoperative anemia. Fifteen minutes later the client reports chills, low back pain, and chest tightness. Which action is the priority?

options.text: Stop the transfusion and keep the IV line open with normal saline.
Slow the transfusion rate and reassess in 5 minutes.
Administer acetaminophen and continue the transfusion.
Document the symptoms as an expected febrile response.

questions.rationale: Chills, low back pain, and chest tightness shortly after starting blood are red-flag findings for an acute transfusion reaction. The nurse should stop the blood immediately to prevent further exposure and maintain IV access with normal saline using new tubing so emergency treatment can be given. Slowing the infusion or masking symptoms delays the response to a potentially life-threatening complication.

distractorRationales.b: This choice focuses on Slow the transfusion rate and reassess in 5 minutes, although the nurse should be guided by A nurse begins a packed red blood cell transfusion for a client with postoperative anemia. Treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, so The safer answer is Stop the transfusion and keep the IV line open with normal saline because delaying circulation and perfusion support can worsen circulation or perfusion.
distractorRationales.c: The clinical error in Administer acetaminophen and continue the transfusion is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, even though the higher-risk finding is chest tightness shortly after starting blood are red-flag findings for an acute transfusion reaction. Stop the transfusion and keep the IV line open with normal saline is safer because it addresses circulation or perfusion before routine care or reassurance.
distractorRationales.d: The problem with Document the symptoms as an expected febrile response is that it normalizes a finding that should change the nurse's priority, while the decisive cue is Chills, low back pain. The nurse should prioritize Stop the transfusion and keep the IV line open with normal saline; otherwise circulation or perfusion remains the active threat.

questions.takeaway: Suspected transfusion reaction means stop the blood first and keep the line open with normal saline.

questions.stem: A client with profuse diarrhea tests positive for Clostridioides difficile. Which infection-control action should the nurse implement?

options.text: Use soap and water for hand hygiene after removing gloves.
Use alcohol-based sanitizer alone because it is faster.
Place the client on droplet precautions only.
Share the blood pressure cuff after wiping it with a dry towel.

questions.rationale: C. difficile spores are not reliably removed by alcohol-based hand rub alone, so soap-and-water hand hygiene is required after glove removal. Contact precautions and dedicated equipment are also important. Using only sanitizer or shared equipment increases transmission risk.

distractorRationales.b: The clinical error in Use alcohol-based sanitizer alone because it is faster is that it anchors on the wrong explanation for the client's cues, when the bedside priority is driven by c diff spore precautions infection. Use soap and water for hand hygiene after removing gloves is safer because it addresses infection progression before routine care or reassurance.
distractorRationales.c: The problem with Place the client on droplet precautions only is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, although the nurse should be guided by c diff spore precautions infection. The nurse should prioritize Use soap and water for hand hygiene after removing gloves; otherwise infection progression remains the active threat.
distractorRationales.d: The reasoning trap in Share the blood pressure cuff after wiping it with a dry towel is that it anchors on the wrong explanation for the client's cues, and misses that c diff spore precautions infection. It is less defensible because Use soap and water for hand hygiene after removing gloves targets the problem most likely to deteriorate first.

questions.takeaway: C. difficile care requires soap-and-water hand hygiene and contact precautions.

questions.stem: Which action by the nurse best helps prevent a central line-associated bloodstream infection in a hospitalized client?

options.text: Scrub the hub before each access and maintain a sterile dressing.
Change the dressing only when the client reports discomfort.
Use the line for routine blood draws to avoid extra venipunctures.
Leave one port uncapped between intermittent infusions.

questions.rationale: Consistent hub disinfection and sterile dressing care are core nursing actions for preventing central line infections. Using the line casually or leaving it uncapped increases contamination risk. Dressing changes should follow evidence-based timing and contamination rules, not patient comfort alone.

distractorRationales.b: The reasoning trap in Change the dressing only when the client reports discomfort is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, when the bedside priority is driven by Preventing CLABSI depends on aseptic access and sterile site care every time. The correct path is Scrub the hub before each access and maintain a sterile dressing, which matches the priority cues instead of a secondary concern.
distractorRationales.c: This answer choice treats Use the line for routine blood draws to avoid extra venipunctures as the key issue, instead of responding to Consistent hub disinfection and sterile dressing care are core nursing actions for preventing central line infections. Treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, so The priority is Scrub the hub before each access and maintain a sterile dressing, not a step that delays stabilization of infection progression.
distractorRationales.d: Leave one port uncapped between intermittent infusions would not address the priority, and misses that Preventing CLABSI depends on aseptic access and sterile site care every time. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so Scrub the hub before each access and maintain a sterile dressing is safer because it addresses infection progression before routine care or reassurance.

questions.takeaway: Preventing CLABSI depends on aseptic access and sterile site care every time.

questions.stem: A nurse caring for a client with a chest tube notes continuous bubbling in the water-seal chamber. What is the nurse's priority action?

options.text: Check the tubing and drainage system connections for an air leak.
Clamp the chest tube near the client's chest for 30 minutes.
Add more sterile water to the suction-control chamber.
Strip the tubing toward the drainage system.

questions.rationale: Continuous bubbling in the water-seal chamber suggests an air leak somewhere in the system. The nurse should first assess connections and tubing to locate the leak while keeping the system functional. Prolonged clamping can cause a tension pneumothorax, and stripping the tubing is not recommended because it can increase intrathoracic pressure.

distractorRationales.b: The problem with Clamp the chest tube near the client's chest for 30 minutes is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, rather than acting on The nurse should first assess connections and tubing to locate the leak while keeping the system functional. That mismatch risks slower recognition of circulation or perfusion and postpones the needed nursing response.
distractorRationales.c: Add more sterile water to the suction-control chamber fits a lower-risk interpretation, although the nurse should be guided by The nurse should first assess connections and tubing to locate the leak while keeping the system functional. Moves to a lower-acuity action before the client is clinically stable, so That mismatch risks slower recognition of circulation or perfusion and postpones the needed nursing response.
distractorRationales.d: The clinical error in Strip the tubing toward the drainage system is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, and misses that The nurse should first assess connections and tubing to locate the leak while keeping the system functional. The correct path is Check the tubing and drainage system connections for an air leak, which matches the priority cues instead of a secondary concern.

questions.takeaway: Continuous water-seal bubbling means assess the system for an air leak before doing anything that could trap air in the chest.

questions.stem: A client with a short leg cast reports increasing pain that is not relieved by opioids. The toes are pale and the client reports numbness. Which action is the priority?

options.text: Notify the provider immediately about possible compartment syndrome.
Apply ice over the cast and reassess after the next pain dose.
Elevate the leg above the level of the heart.
Loosen the cast with scissors at the bedside.

questions.rationale: Pain out of proportion, pallor, and numbness suggest compartment syndrome, which can threaten tissue viability and limb function. Immediate provider notification and urgent evaluation are needed. Comfort-only measures or excessive elevation can worsen ischemia or delay definitive treatment.

distractorRationales.b: The problem with Apply ice over the cast and reassess after the next pain dose is that it treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, while the decisive cue is numbness suggest compartment syndrome, which can threaten tissue viability and limb function. That mismatch risks slower recognition of medication toxicity and postpones the needed nursing response.
distractorRationales.c: This answer choice treats Elevate the leg above the level of the heart as the key issue, while the decisive cue is Pain out of proportion, pallor. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The priority is Notify the provider immediately about possible compartment syndrome, not a step that delays stabilization of medication toxicity.
distractorRationales.d: The clinical error in Loosen the cast with scissors at the bedside is that it anchors on the wrong explanation for the client's cues, but the stem cue is A client with a short leg cast reports increasing pain that is not relieved by opioids. Notify the provider immediately about possible compartment syndrome better matches the nursing priority, so this option would slow the needed intervention.

questions.takeaway: Pain that outpaces the injury plus pallor or numbness under a cast should trigger concern for compartment syndrome.
